claim: DKOM rootkit: Active process list (EPROCESS ActiveProcessLinks) has been unlinked. vol_pslist and vol_pstree return zero rows, while vol_psscan finds 101 processes via pool scanning. Likewise, vol_cmdline, vol_dlllist, vol_malfind, and vol_svcscan all return zero rows — consistent with kernel-level process hiding.
rationale: vol_pslist and vol_pstree return 0 rows (confirmed empty outputs). vol_psscan returns 101 processes including active ones like System(4), services.exe(536), lsass.exe(544), and the suspicious subject_srv.ex(6160). Only pool-scanning bypasses the DKOM unlink. All plugins that walk the EPROCESS list (cmdline, dlllist, malfind, svcscan) also return 0 rows, confirming the linked list is severed.

claim: Backdoor process "subject_srv.ex" (PID 6160): Running as a child of services.exe (PPID 536), 32-bit (Wow64) on 64-bit OS, 12 threads, created 2018-09-06 19:25:36. Listening on TCP port 3262 (0.0.0.0 and ::), with an ESTABLISHED C2 connection to 172.16.5.50:44262.
rationale: vol_psscan shows PID 6160 "subject_srv.ex" with PPID 536 (services.exe), ExitTime null (still alive), Wow64 true, SessionId 0, 12 threads. vol_netscan shows it listening on TCP port 3262 (Offset 2077872576), and has an ESTABLISHED connection to 172.16.5.50:44262 (Offset 2075192592). The truncated name (13 chars) is typical of Windows EPROCESS ImageFileName field but the unusual name "subject_srv" is not a legitimate Windows process.

claim: PowerShell C2 chain: powershell.exe (PID 4072) was spawned by WmiPrvSE.exe (PID 1196) on 2018-08-28 22:08:25. It then spawned a 32-bit powershell.exe (PID 3164, Wow64), which spawned dozens of short-lived rundll32.exe processes across Aug 30–Sep 6 2018. Both PowerShell processes have TCP connections to 172.16.4.10:8080 — PID 4072 (CLOSED) and PID 3164 (CLOSE_WAIT).
rationale: vol_psscan: PID 4072 powershell.exe (parent 1196 WmiPrvSE.exe, created 2018-08-28 22:08:25, 12 threads, ExitTime null); PID 3164 powershell.exe (parent 4072, created 2018-08-28 22:08:26, 8 threads, Wow64 true, ExitTime null). PID 3164 spawned 25+ rundll32.exe instances (PIDs: 4496, 3780, 2972, 4948, 5440, 6036, 6004, 5296, 2476, 4632, 4580, 5872, 4292, 4376, 4688, 4264, 2420, 3376, 3548, 300, 3260, 6088, 3500, 5892, 252, 4984, 5640, 6648) each with 0 threads and short lifespan. vol_netscan: PID 3164 has CLOSE_WAIT to 172.16.4.10:8080; PID 4072 has CLOSED to 172.16.4.10:8080.

claim: Rundll32 beaconing/execution pattern: The 32-bit powershell.exe (PID 3164) spawned at least 28 rundll32.exe processes in rapid bursts across multiple days (Aug 30, Aug 31, Sep 5, Sep 6 2018). Each rundll32.exe ran for only 2–5 seconds and had 0 threads at capture time, consistent with a C2 beacon or staged payload execution model.
rationale: vol_psscan documents 28 rundll32.exe processes all parented by PID 3164 (powershell.exe) with lifespans of 2–5 seconds each. Example: PID 4496 created 2018-08-31 22:03:56, exited 22:04:01 (5s); PID 5440 created 22:06:15, exited 22:06:19 (4s); PID 5296 created 22:10:39, exited 22:10:43 (4s). The rapid, repetitive execution from a PowerShell process is classic C2 beacon behavior.

claim: Common C2 infrastructure at 172.16.4.10:8080: At least three separate processes connected to this IP/port — powershell.exe PID 3164 (CLOSE_WAIT), powershell.exe PID 4072 (CLOSED), and ngentask.exe PID 7092 (two CLOSED connections, ephemeral ports 56322 and 56324). This indicates a shared C2 server used by multiple compromised processes.
rationale: vol_netscan shows: PID 3164 (powershell.exe) TCPv4 172.16.4.5:56932 → 172.16.4.10:8080 state CLOSE_WAIT (Offset 2075073808); PID 4072 (powershell.exe) TCPv4 172.16.4.5:54794 → 172.16.4.10:8080 state CLOSED (Offset 2077753360); PID 7092 (ngentask.exe) TCPv4 172.16.4.5:56322 → 172.16.4.10:8080 state CLOSED (Offset 1417783168) and 172.16.4.5:56324 → 172.16.4.10:8080 state CLOSED (Offset 918788800). Three distinct compromised binaries (two PowerShell, one ngentask) reaching the same C2 port.

claim: Suspicious Uninstall.exe execution: cmd.exe (PID 4808) spawned Uninstall.exe (PID 2340) on 2018-09-06 16:36:33. The binary ran for only 2 seconds and made a TCP connection to 172.16.7.12:135 (RPC endpoint mapper) before exiting — consistent with lateral movement or reconnaissance.
rationale: vol_psscan: PID 2340 "Uninstall.exe", PPID 4808 (cmd.exe), created 2018-09-06 16:36:33, exited 16:36:35 (2s lifespan). vol_netscan: PID 2340 TCPv4 172.16.4.5:58810 → 172.16.7.12:135 state CLOSED. Port 135 is the Microsoft RPC endpoint mapper, commonly targeted for lateral movement. The short lifespan and unusual execution chain (cmd→Uninstall.exe) is suspicious.

claim: WMI-based persistence: The initial PowerShell C2 process (PID 4072, powershell.exe) was spawned by WmiPrvSE.exe (PID 1196) on 2018-08-28 22:08:25. WmiPrvSE.exe is the WMI Provider Host, and spawning PowerShell from it is a well-known technique for WMI event subscription persistence (e.g., __EventFilter / __EventConsumer bindings).
rationale: vol_psscan: PID 4072 powershell.exe has PPID 1196 (WmiPrvSE.exe), created 2018-08-28 22:08:25, still alive with 12 threads. WmiPrvSE.exe normally hosts WMI providers, not user-mode PowerShell. The parent-child relationship from WmiPrvSE to PowerShell is a strong indicator of a WMI event subscription triggering code execution, a common persistence mechanism used by APT actors.

claim: Suspicious ngentask.exe C2 connections: ngentask.exe (PID 7092, parent PID 2536) ran briefly on 2018-09-06 07:27:10–07:27:11 and made two TCP connections to 172.16.4.10:8080 (the shared C2 server). While ngentask.exe is a legitimate .NET NGEN task binary, its use of the C2 channel suggests it was either hijacked or is a renamed malicious binary.
rationale: vol_psscan: PID 7092 "ngentask.exe", PPID 2536 (not identified as live), created 2018-09-06 07:27:10, exited 07:27:11, SessionId 0. vol_netscan: Two TCP CLOSED connections from ngentask.exe to 172.16.4.10:8080 (offsets 1417783168, 918788800), the same C2 server contacted by the PowerShell chain.

claim: ngentask.exe (PID 7092, PPID 2536) established two TCP connections to 172.16.4.10:8080 — the same IP:port used by powershell.exe C2 processes (PIDs 3164 and 4072). The connections are correlated with malicious C2 infrastructure, but no direct evidence confirms the ngentask.exe binary itself was hijacked, replaced, or otherwise tampered with.
rationale: vol_psscan confirms ngentask.exe (PID 7092, PPID 2536) existed briefly on 2018-09-06 07:27:10–07:27:11 UTC in Session 0. vol_netscan shows two TCPv4 connections from PID 7092 to 172.16.4.10:8080 (local ports 56322 and 56324, both CLOSED). The same 172.16.4.10:8080 server is contacted by powershell.exe PID 3164 (connection state CLOSE_WAIT, local port 56932) and powershell.exe PID 4072 (connection CLOSED, local port 54794). PID 3164 — a Wow64 powershell process with PPID 4072, alive since 2018-08-28 — spawned 28+ short-lived rundll32.exe children (2–21 sec each), a strong behavioral indicator of C2 staging/injection. The ngentask.exe connections therefore share the same C2 channel as the powershell actors. However, the image is severely degraded (pslist, pstree, cmdline, malfind, dlllist, svcscan all empty), so binary-level confirmation (code injection, command line, DLL load) is unavailable for PID 7092. Parent PID 2536 is unidentified, and both ngentask connections are CLOSED with no creation timestamp, so precise temporal linkage to the 1-second process window cannot be confirmed. The correlation is suggestive but not definitive for binary tampering.

claim: ngentask.exe (PID 7092) established two TCP connections to 172.16.4.10:8080 within its 1-second lifespan — the same destination IP:port contacted by two long-running powershell.exe processes (PIDs 3164 and 4072). powershell.exe PID 4072 was spawned via WMI (parent WmiPrvSE.exe PID 1196); its child powershell.exe PID 3164 spawned approximately 28 short-lived rundll32.exe instances (2–38 seconds each) over 7 days. The destination 172.16.4.10:8080 is contacted exclusively by these three processes. ngentask.exe — a .NET compilation tool — has no legitimate operational reason for outbound HTTP connections. While cmdline and DLL data are unavailable to confirm C2 commands, the aggregate behavioral pattern is highly anomalous and consistent with attacker activity.
rationale: vol_netscan confirms ngentask.exe (PID 7092) made two TCP connections to 172.16.4.10:8080 (local ports 56322, 56324), and powershell.exe PIDs 3164 (local port 56932, CLOSE_WAIT) and 4072 (local port 54794, CLOSED) also connected to the same destination. No other process contacts 172.16.4.10. vol_psscan shows ngentask.exe lived only 1 second (Create 2018-09-06T07:27:10, Exit 2018-09-06T07:27:11); powershell.exe PID 4072 was spawned by WmiPrvSE.exe (PID 1196); powershell.exe PID 3164 (child of 4072) spawned ~28 rundll32.exe children with 2–38s lifespans across Aug 30–Sep 6. The combination of ngentask.exe's anomalous network activity, its 1-second lifespan, the WMI-spawned PowerShell parentage, and the rundll32 spawning chain supports compromise assessment. However, cmdline/dlllist/malfind all return empty, so the C2 interpretation cannot be conclusively proven; alternative explanations (e.g., internal web service) remain possible, capping confidence at 0.70.
